summary: Gruber addresses the outcry from iPhone users who felt 'screwed over' when Apple's 1.1.1 update bricked jailbroken and unlocked iPhones. He argues that the fundamental misunderstanding stems from a sense of entitlement: users expect to do unsupported things and still receive support. He draws a distinction between hardware modifications, where constraints are visible and physical, and software modifications, where constraints are invisible and based on policy rather than physics. He concludes that regardless of whether Apple's decisions are rooted in marketing or engineering, the vendor sets the terms of support, and users who hack their devices must accept responsibility for the consequences.
key_points: Users who modified their iPhones felt entitled to continued support despite taking explicitly unsupported actions
Hardware constraints are visible and physical (can't), while software constraints are invisible and policy-based (won't), which is why people resist accepting them
The distinction between can't and won't doesn't change the vendor's right to define what is and isn't supported
Hacking your own device is your right, but expecting the vendor to support your hacks is not
money_quotes: What it boils down to is this: a sense of entitlement that users should be able to do unsupported things and yet still be supported.
With hardware, the constraints are visible. The limitations of what is supported and unsupported are issues of can't.
With software, though, the constraints are invisible and arbitrary. The limitations regarding what is and is not supported are issues of won't rather than can't.
That this support decision is perhaps based on nothing more than marketing rather than being rooted in the laws of physics doesn't change the fact that the decision was made.
The decision as to what is supported and what is not lies with the vendor, not the customer.
The point is that if you hack, you're on your own. You can't do unsupported things and expect to be supported for them just because you think these actions should be supported.
key_insight: The invisible, policy-based nature of software constraints leads people to mistake 'won't support' for 'should support,' but choosing to hack means choosing to be on your own.
